title: Digital Credit: How MicroStrategy Wants to Give a Billion People a 10% Bank Account

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
Bitcoin has delivered annualized returns of roughly 39% over the past five years — more than double the S&P 500. Yet fewer than five people in a hundred will accept it when pitched, because its volatility runs at approximately 40 times that of a money market fund. The performance is extraordinary, but the ride is unbearable for most. Retirees, conservative savers, and anyone who needs to pay bills in the next two to three years simply cannot stomach four-year drawdowns.

This is the problem that digital credit instruments aim to solve. The concept is straightforward: MicroStrategy runs what amounts to a "crypto reactor" — a $60 billion Bitcoin treasury that captures the full volatility and upside. From that reactor, the company issues credit instruments that strip away the volatility and pass through a steady yield of roughly 10-11.5% per year, over-collateralized at approximately 5x by the underlying Bitcoin. The risk reduces to two factors: Bitcoin going to zero permanently, or the management team failing to competently steward the treasury.

The market dynamics are revealing. If you ask a hundred people on the street whether they want a bank account paying 10-11%, virtually everyone says yes. If you ask those same hundred people whether they want a highly volatile crypto asset with no cash flows and a four-to-ten-year time horizon, maybe two will agree — and only after a hundred hours of discussion. The market for the first 11% of Bitcoin's return is roughly 100 times larger than the market for the full 40%.

A key structural advantage is tax treatment. Because the underlying Bitcoin is held indefinitely as an unrealized capital gain, the dividends paid to credit investors qualify as return-of-capital distributions — meaning they are tax-deferred. The first $100 of dividends per share accrues without triggering a tax event, and if the instrument passes to an heir, they receive a step-up in basis, resetting the clock. Over 20 years, the compounding benefit of tax-deferred reinvestment at 11% dramatically outperforms conventional taxable credit instruments.

The carry trade opportunity compounds the appeal further. Institutional borrowers who can access capital at SOFR plus 50-100 basis points — roughly 4% — can invest at 11% and pocket the 7-8% spread. For retail investors, it represents something simpler: a path where plugging 7% of the yield back into the instrument preserves purchasing power against debasement, while the remaining 4.5% funds living expenses. It is, in essence, the nuclear reactor model applied to finance — most people don't want to run the reactor, but everyone wants the electricity.
